past_medical_history: Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss diagnosed 3 years ago, wears hearing aids
Hypertension diagnosed at age 62, well-controlled on medication
Benign prostatic hyperplasia diagnosed at age 67
No history of head trauma or seizures
family_history: Mother had dementia diagnosed at age 78, passed away at age 84
Father died of myocardial infarction at age 72
One older brother, age 75, with mild cognitive impairment
No family history of Parkinson's disease or psychiatric illness

social_history.occupation: Retired middle school teacher (retired 2 years ago due to hearing difficulties)
social_history.smoking: Former smoker, quit 20 years ago (15 pack-year history)
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in their own home. Wife is primary informant and caregiver.

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person. Oriented to place (correctly identifies the hospital). Oriented to month and season but states the year incorrectly (off by one year). Cannot recall the day of the week. MMSE score 22/30: lost 2 points on orientation to time, 1 point on orientation to place (floor), 3 points on delayed recall (0/3 objects at 5 minutes), 1 point on serial sevens, 1 point on sentence writing. Clock drawing test shows mildly disorganized number placement with hands indicating an incorrect time. Affect is pleasant but occasionally perplexed. Speech is fluent but with intermittent word-finding pauses. Comprehension intact for simple commands but difficulty with multi-step instructions.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light (3mm to 2mm bilaterally). Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Facial strength symmetric. Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss (uses hearing aids). Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift. No tremor, rigidity, or bradykinesia.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and proprioception intact in all extremities. Vibratory sensation mildly reduced at bilateral great toes, consistent with age.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No frontal release signs (palmomental, grasp, or snout reflexes absent).
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal gait. Tandem walk intact. Romberg negative. No shuffling, festination, or magnetic gait.
neurological_exam.additional: During the interview, the patient repeated the same question about the time of his next appointment three times within 10 minutes, apparently unaware he had already asked. His wife reports that he has been increasingly forgetful over the past 18-24 months, frequently misplaces items, forgets recent conversations, and has difficulty following television plotlines. He recently forgot a long-standing appointment with a friend and left the stove on twice. He has stopped managing the household finances, a task he previously handled independently. He occasionally appears confused about the day of the week and has expressed frustration about his memory, stating 'I feel like my mind is slipping away.' His wife also notes that he has become more socially withdrawn since retirement.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive memory loss over the past 18 to 24 months with increasing forgetfulness and difficulty managing daily tasks
patient.history_present_illness: A 71-year-old right-handed retired male middle school teacher presents to the neurology outpatient clinic accompanied by his wife for evaluation of progressive memory difficulties. His wife, who serves as the primary informant, reports that she first noticed subtle changes approximately 18 to 24 months ago, shortly after his retirement. He retired at age 69 primarily because of progressive bilateral hearing loss, but his wife notes that the memory difficulties became apparent around the same time and have been gradually worsening independent of his hearing issues. The earliest symptoms included repeating questions he had just asked, forgetting the content of recent conversations, and misplacing everyday items such as his wallet and keys. Over the following months, the memory lapses became more frequent and noticeable. He began forgetting appointments, including a longstanding monthly lunch with a former colleague. On two occasions in the past six months, he left the gas stove on after cooking, which prompted significant concern from his wife. He previously managed all of the household finances, including bill payments and tax preparation, but approximately eight months ago his wife discovered several unpaid utility bills and took over these responsibilities. He has difficulty following the plots of television programs and often asks what is happening during shows he has watched from the beginning. He frequently loses track of the day of the week and has occasionally appeared confused about the time of day, remarking that time seems to move strangely. His wife also describes a change in his personality: he has become more withdrawn and apathetic, declining invitations from friends and spending more time sitting quietly at home. He has expressed frustration and awareness of his declining memory, telling his wife on multiple occasions that he feels as though he is losing his mind. He continues to dress and bathe independently, though his wife has noticed he occasionally wears the same clothes on consecutive days. He can still prepare simple meals and navigate familiar routes in the neighborhood without getting lost, though his wife recently began accompanying him on errands as a precaution. There is no history of sudden stepwise decline, fluctuating awareness, visual hallucinations, falls, tremor, or gait disturbance. He has not experienced any episodes of confusion associated with fever or illness. He denies depressed mood, though his wife notes the social withdrawal. He sleeps well without evidence of REM sleep behavior disorder. There have been no episodes of urinary incontinence. His appetite has remained stable.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7.5-8 Hz alpha rhythm, bilaterally symmetric but slower than expected for age. Reactive to eye opening.
background.anterior_rhythm: Low-voltage beta activity in frontal regions, within normal limits
background.sleep_architecture: Brief drowsiness captured; no sleep recorded

findings.state: Present during wakefulness, accentuated during drowsiness

artifacts.impact: Intermittent, did not significantly obscure underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Mild increase in generalized theta slowing during 3-minute hyperventilation, no epileptiform discharges provoked
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG for age. The posterior dominant rhythm is slowed at 7.5-8 Hz with intermittent generalized theta activity. No epileptiform discharges or seizures were recorded. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording. No sleep captured beyond brief drowsiness. Longer recording or sleep-deprived EEG could provide additional information.

findings.size: Scheltens visual rating scale grade 2 bilaterally (left slightly more prominent than right)

signal_characteristics.T1: Volume loss with widened choroidal fissure and temporal horn dilation bilaterally
signal_characteristics.T2: No abnormal signal within hippocampal formations
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: No abnormal signal within hippocampal formations

findings.location: Bilateral temporoparietal cortex, more prominent than expected for age

signal_characteristics.T1: Widened sulci and mild ventricular enlargement, temporoparietal predominant

findings.location: Periventricular and scattered deep white matter

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Scattered punctate periventricular and deep white matter hyperintensities

volumetrics.left_hippocampal_volume: 2.62 cm³ (8th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.right_hippocampal_volume: 2.84 cm³ (14th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.total_brain_volume: 1085 cm³ (18th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.temporoparietal_cortical_thickness: Reduced bilaterally, left > right, below 10th percentile

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No mass lesions or abnormal enhancement post-gadolinium
No evidence of normal pressure hydrocephalus (ventricular enlargement proportionate to sulcal widening)
Mild mucosal thickening in bilateral maxillary sinuses (incidental)
mri.impression: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 2, left slightly greater than right) with corresponding temporal horn dilation. Temporoparietal-predominant cortical atrophy beyond what is expected for age. Mild small vessel white matter disease (Fazekas grade 1), likely age-related and not of sufficient severity to account for cognitive symptoms. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Mild left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (borderline)
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with borderline voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy, likely related to chronic hypertension. No acute abnormalities. No arrhythmia identified.

abnormal_values_summary: LDL: 112 mg/dL (mildly elevated, reference <100 mg/dL) — not related to cognitive symptoms, consider statin dose adjustment (H)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: MMSE 22 /30 (L), MoCA 18 /30 (L), Delayed Recall (CVLT-II) 3rd percentile percentile (H), Recognition Memory 12th percentile percentile (H), Category Fluency (Animals) 10 words/minute (L), Boston Naming Test 15th percentile percentile (H).

signal_characteristics.cortical_uptake: Elevated cortical tracer retention in bilateral frontal, parietal, temporal, and posterior cingulate cortices

additional_observations: White matter uptake is within normal limits for age
No focal areas of asymmetric uptake to suggest focal pathology
output.impression: Positive amyloid PET scan with diffuse cortical amyloid deposition. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Bilateral temporal and parietal cortices, posterior cingulate cortex, and precuneus

signal_characteristics.temporal_cortex: Bilateral temporoparietal hypometabolism, left greater than right (15-20% reduction compared to frontal cortex)
signal_characteristics.posterior_cingulate: Markedly reduced metabolism in posterior cingulate and precuneus

additional_observations: Cerebellar metabolism is symmetric and normal
output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates bilateral temporoparietal and posterior cingulate/precuneus hypometabolism with relative frontal sparing. This is the characteristic metabolic pattern of Alzheimer's disease. The asymmetry (left > right) is concordant with the clinical predominance of verbal memory and language difficulties. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Neurofilament light chain (NfL): 1250 pg/mL (mildly elevated, reference <1000 pg/mL for age)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 14 cmH2O. WBC: 1 cell/µL (normal) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 38 mg/dL (normal, reference 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 62 mg/dL (normal) (ratio 0.63 (normal, reference >0.6)). Amyloid-beta 42: 385 pg/mL (decreased, reference >500 pg/mL). Amyloid-beta 40: 8200 pg/mL (normal). Amyloid-beta 42/40 ratio: 0.047 (decreased, reference >0.062). Phosphorylated tau (p-tau 181): 78 pg/mL (elevated, reference <60 pg/mL). Total tau (t-tau): 485 pg/mL (elevated, reference <400 pg/mL). Neurofilament light chain (NfL): 1250 pg/mL (mildly elevated, reference <1000 pg/mL for age).

abnormal_values_summary: APOE genotype: ε3/ε4 (heterozygous carrier of the ε4 risk allele) (H)

output.query: early Alzheimer's disease treatment cholinesterase inhibitors disease-modifying therapy

results.title: Lecanemab in Early Alzheimer's Disease
results.authors: van Dyck CH, Swanson CJ, et al.
results.summary: Phase 3 trial (CLARITY AD) demonstrated that lecanemab, an anti-amyloid antibody, reduced clinical decline by 27% over 18 months in early AD (MCI to mild dementia) compared to placebo. ARIA (amyloid-related imaging abnormalities) occurred in 21.3% of lecanemab-treated patients. FDA approved January 2023.

results.title: Donepezil and Memantine for Moderate-to-Severe Alzheimer's Disease
results.summary: DOMINO-AD trial showed continued benefit of donepezil in moderate-to-severe AD. Starting donepezil in early disease remains standard of care. Memantine showed additional benefit when added to donepezil in moderate-to-severe stages.

results.title: 2023 Appropriate Use Recommendations for Lecanemab
results.authors: Cummings J, Rabinovici GD, et al.
results.journal: Journal of Prevention of Alzheimer's Disease
results.summary: Appropriate use criteria require biomarker-confirmed amyloid pathology, early-stage disease (MCI or mild dementia), baseline MRI without extensive ARIA risk factors, and informed shared decision-making. APOE ε4 homozygotes and patients on anticoagulants have higher ARIA risk.

output.summary: Standard symptomatic treatment for early AD includes cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine). Disease-modifying anti-amyloid therapies (lecanemab, donanemab) are now available for biomarker-confirmed early AD, with demonstrated slowing of clinical decline. Patient selection requires amyloid PET or CSF biomarker confirmation, baseline MRI, and assessment of ARIA risk factors including APOE genotype.

output.proposed: Donepezil 5 mg once daily at bedtime, titrating to 10 mg after 4-6 weeks
interactions: Donepezil + Amlodipine: No clinically significant interaction. Both are metabolized by CYP3A4 but no dose adjustment needed.
Donepezil + Tamsulosin: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction. Both may cause dizziness; advise caution with positional changes.
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to donepezil or piperidine derivatives
warnings: Cholinergic effects: may cause bradycardia — monitor heart rate, particularly given concurrent amlodipine
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, anorexia) are common, especially during dose titration
May exacerbate urinary obstruction — monitor given history of BPH and concurrent tamsulosin
May lower seizure threshold — monitor clinically
May exacerbate pulmonary conditions due to cholinergic effects — no active pulmonary disease in this patient
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required. Generic available.
alternatives: Rivastigmine patch (4.6 mg/24h, titrate to 13.3 mg/24h) — transdermal delivery may reduce GI side effects
Galantamine ER 8 mg daily, titrate to 16-24 mg — dual mechanism (AChE inhibitor + nicotinic receptor modulator)
Memantine 5 mg daily, titrate to 10 mg twice daily — typically reserved for moderate-to-severe AD, but may be added later

differential.key_features: Progressive amnestic cognitive decline over 18-24 months, bilateral hippocampal atrophy on MRI, impaired episodic memory with encoding deficit on neuropsychological testing, positive family history
Social withdrawal and apathy could suggest depression, but GDS score is normal, cognitive deficits are progressive over 2 years, and the memory pattern shows encoding rather than retrieval deficit

Patient has vascular risk factors (hypertension, hyperlipidemia, smoking history) and mild white matter disease, but Fazekas grade 1 is insufficient to explain symptoms, decline is gradual rather than stepwise, and atrophy pattern is temporoparietal rather than frontal-subcortical

Bilateral hearing loss preceded cognitive complaints and is a known risk factor for dementia, but the progressive course, structural brain changes, and neuropsychological profile point to a neurodegenerative etiology
Although apathy and social withdrawal are present, the dominant symptom is episodic memory loss, not personality change or disinhibition. MRI shows temporoparietal rather than frontotemporal atrophy.
No visual hallucinations, no parkinsonism (no tremor, rigidity, or bradykinesia on exam), no fluctuating cognition, no REM sleep behavior disorder

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI to evaluate for structural causes of cognitive decline and characteristic atrophy patterns
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 2) with temporoparietal cortical volume loss, mild white matter disease

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory panel to rule out reversible causes of dementia (thyroid, B12, syphilis, HIV, metabolic)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal CBC, BMP, thyroid function, B12, folate, RPR, and HIV — excluding reversible causes

optimal_actions.action: Request comprehensive neuropsychological testing to characterize the cognitive profile and severity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Disproportionate episodic memory impairment with encoding deficit, impaired semantic fluency, relatively preserved executive function — consistent with Alzheimer's pattern

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for subclinical seizures or other treatable causes of cognitive dysfunction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Slowed posterior dominant rhythm and diffuse theta slowing without epileptiform discharges

optimal_actions.action: Request CSF biomarkers (amyloid-beta 42, phospho-tau, total tau) for biological confirmation of Alzheimer's pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Decreased Aβ42, decreased Aβ42/40 ratio, elevated phospho-tau and total tau (A+T+N+ profile)

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on current treatment options for early Alzheimer's disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Evidence for cholinesterase inhibitors and emerging disease-modifying anti-amyloid therapies

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for donepezil with the patient's current medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant interactions, but monitoring for bradycardia and urinary symptoms advised

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI to identify hippocampal atrophy and exclude other structural causes (tumor, NPH, stroke)
Obtain laboratory screening for reversible causes of dementia (thyroid function, vitamin B12, RPR, HIV)
Perform or request formal cognitive/neuropsychological testing to characterize the pattern and severity of impairment
Recognize the progressive amnestic presentation with functional decline as consistent with Alzheimer's disease rather than normal aging
Exclude depression as a primary cause of cognitive symptoms (pseudodementia)
contraindicated_actions: Attributing the cognitive decline solely to hearing loss or normal aging without further evaluation
Prescribing anticholinergic medications which would worsen cognitive symptoms
Initiating memantine as first-line monotherapy in mild-stage AD (cholinesterase inhibitors are first-line)
Ordering unnecessary invasive tests (e.g., brain biopsy) when clinical and imaging findings are consistent with AD
Discontinuing the patient's antihypertensive medication — vascular risk factor management is important for dementia care
key_reasoning_points: Progressive episodic memory loss over 18-24 months with functional decline (inability to manage finances, forgetting appointments, leaving stove on) establishes a dementia syndrome, not normal aging or subjective cognitive decline
The memory pattern — repeating questions, forgetting recent conversations, encoding deficit on testing — is characteristic of hippocampal/medial temporal lobe dysfunction seen in Alzheimer's disease
Family history is significant: mother with dementia at 78 and brother with MCI increase the pre-test probability of AD
Normal dementia screening labs (thyroid, B12, RPR, HIV) effectively exclude the most common reversible causes of cognitive decline
MRI showing bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 2) with temporoparietal cortical volume loss is the characteristic neuroimaging pattern of Alzheimer's disease
The mild white matter disease (Fazekas grade 1) is age-appropriate and insufficient to account for the clinical presentation, making vascular dementia unlikely as the primary diagnosis
Hearing loss is an established modifiable risk factor for dementia but does not explain the structural brain changes or the specific amnestic cognitive profile

Although social withdrawal and apathy are present, the absence of significant depressive symptoms on screening (GDS 4/15) and the progressive course with structural brain changes argue against pseudodementia

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic progressive amnestic presentation, clear bilateral hippocampal atrophy on MRI, normal reversible dementia screening labs, and supportive family history. Minimal confounders — hearing loss and mild white matter disease are present but do not obscure the diagnosis.
